county_metadata.jurisdictional_notes: Allen County formed 1855 as one of the original Kansas Territory counties. County seat is Iola, named for the wife of an early settler. Located in the Neosho River valley in southeastern Kansas. Agriculture and natural gas production were primary economic drivers. Population peaked in the early 1900s during the natural gas boom.
county_metadata.research_tips: Allen County Register of Deeds in Iola holds land records from 1855. The Allen County Historical Society maintains local genealogical collections including cemetery transcriptions and family files. Probate Court records date from the county's organization. The county was an early settlement area along the Neosho River with records at KSHS in Topeka. Church records from Methodist and Baptist congregations established in the late 1850s are important for vital events predating state registration (1911). FamilySearch has microfilmed Allen County deed books, probate records, and some marriage records. BLM GLO land patents document original federal land entries in the county.
county_metadata.online_land_records_scope: BLM GLO federal land patents for Allen County; county deed books at the Register of Deeds office in Iola

county_metadata.record_losses: Courthouse fire 1870 destroyed early deed and probate records
county_metadata.jurisdictional_notes: Anderson County formed 1855 as an original Kansas Territory county. County seat is Garnett. The 1870 courthouse fire destroyed early records. Located in eastern Kansas, the county was part of the Bleeding Kansas border conflict zone. Strong Free State and abolitionist sentiment. Named for Joseph C. Anderson, a member of the territorial legislature.
county_metadata.research_tips: Anderson County courthouse fire of 1870 destroyed early deed and probate records; researchers should check KSHS in Topeka for territorial-era records, BLM GLO for federal land patents, and FamilySearch for any surviving microfilmed records. The Anderson County Register of Deeds in Garnett holds post-1870 land records. Probate Court records resume after the fire. The county was a Free State stronghold with ties to abolitionist John Brown -- check KSHS manuscript collections for territorial-era correspondence. Anderson County Historical Society in Garnett maintains cemetery indexes and local family histories. The Garnett newspapers from the 1870s onward at KSHS and Newspapers.com can fill gaps from destroyed records.
county_metadata.online_land_records_scope: BLM GLO federal land patents for Anderson County; county deed books at the Register of Deeds office in Garnett

county_metadata.jurisdictional_notes: Atchison County formed 1855 as an original Kansas Territory county. County seat is Atchison, a Missouri River city that was one of the largest in early Kansas. Named for US Senator David Rice Atchison of Missouri. Major steamboat landing, railroad hub (Atchison, Topeka and Santa Fe Railroad headquarters originally here), and commercial center.
county_metadata.research_tips: Atchison County Register of Deeds holds land records from 1855. The city of Atchison was a major Missouri River steamboat landing and railroad hub, making city directories and business records at the Atchison County Historical Society particularly valuable. Atchison was also a key point on the Atchison, Topeka and Santa Fe Railroad -- employee records may be at KSHS. Benedictine College archives hold Catholic parish records from the region. Strong Irish and German immigrant communities produced church records from the 1850s. Probate Court records date from 1855. The Atchison Daily Globe newspaper (from 1877) at KSHS and Newspapers.com has extensive legal notices and obituaries. FamilySearch has microfilmed county records.
county_metadata.online_land_records_scope: BLM GLO federal land patents for Atchison County; county deed books at the Register of Deeds office in Atchison

county_metadata.jurisdictional_notes: Barber County formed 1867, organized 1873. County seat is Medicine Lodge. Named for Thomas W. Barber, a Free State settler killed in 1855. The Treaty of Medicine Lodge (1867) with Southern Plains tribes was signed in the county. Carry Nation began her temperance movement here. Cattle ranching and wheat farming county.
county_metadata.research_tips: Barber County Register of Deeds in Medicine Lodge holds land records from the county's organization in 1873 (practical settlement). Medicine Lodge was famous for the 1867 Treaty of Medicine Lodge with Southern Plains tribes -- NARA holds treaty and Indian agency records. Carry Nation began her temperance crusade in Medicine Lodge; related records at KSHS. Barber County Historical Museum has local family files and photographs. BLM GLO federal land patents document homestead entries. Church records from pioneer congregations (1870s-1880s) are at local churches and some microfilmed by FamilySearch. Probate Court records and District Court naturalizations are at the courthouse. Cattlemen's records from the open-range era may survive at KSHS.
county_metadata.online_land_records_scope: BLM GLO federal land patents for Barber County; county deed books at the Register of Deeds office in Medicine Lodge

county_metadata.jurisdictional_notes: Barton County formed 1867, organized 1872. County seat is Great Bend. Named for Clara Barton, founder of the American Red Cross. Located on the Santa Fe Trail and Chisholm Trail. German-Russian (Volga German) settlers arrived in the 1870s-1880s. Fort Zarah military post operated 1864-1869.
county_metadata.research_tips: Barton County Register of Deeds in Great Bend holds land records from 1872. Great Bend was a cattle town on the Santa Fe Trail and Chisholm Trail -- town records and newspapers document the cattle trade era. The Barton County Historical Society and Museum in Great Bend has local genealogical collections. KSHS holds Barton County newspapers from the 1870s. German-Russian (Volga German) immigrants settled heavily in Barton County in the 1870s-1880s, bringing parish records from Russia -- check Catholic and Lutheran church records in Hoisington and Albert areas. BLM GLO land patents. Probate Court records date from the 1870s. FamilySearch has microfilmed Barton County deed and probate records. Fort Zarah military post records (1864-1869) at NARA may document early settlers.
county_metadata.online_land_records_scope: BLM GLO federal land patents for Barton County; county deed books at the Register of Deeds office in Great Bend

county_metadata.record_losses: Border war raids (1856-1861) destroyed early settlement records; some records lost in courthouse fire 1863
county_metadata.jurisdictional_notes: Bourbon County formed 1855 as an original Kansas Territory county. County seat is Fort Scott. Named for Bourbon County, Kentucky. Severely affected by border war violence (1856-1861). Fort Scott was a significant military post. Important African American community including Exodusters and Buffalo Soldiers' families.
county_metadata.research_tips: Bourbon County suffered significant record losses from border war raids (1856-1861) and an 1863 courthouse fire. Fort Scott was a major military post and Free State stronghold. The Fort Scott National Historic Site and the Bourbon County Historical Society hold supplementary records. Post-war land records at the Register of Deeds in Fort Scott. KSHS holds territorial- era documents and newspapers. BLM GLO land patents fill gaps in destroyed county land records. Fort Scott's African American community (Exodusters, Buffalo Soldiers' families) is documented in church records, freedmen's records, and military pension files at NARA. Crawford Seminary records (Quaker mission to Osage) may be at KSHS. FamilySearch has microfilmed surviving records.
county_metadata.online_land_records_scope: BLM GLO federal land patents for Bourbon County; county deed books at the Register of Deeds office in Fort Scott

BLM GLO federal land patents supplement destroyed county deed books. Register of Deeds holds surviving and post-loss records

county_metadata.jurisdictional_notes: Brown County formed 1855 as an original Kansas Territory county. County seat is Hiawatha. Named for Albert Gallatin Brown, US Senator from Mississippi. Iowa, Sac & Fox, and Kickapoo reservation lands partially within the county. Missouri River corridor settlement.
county_metadata.research_tips: Brown County Register of Deeds in Hiawatha holds land records from 1855. The county includes portions of the Iowa and Sac & Fox reservations -- tribal records at NARA Kansas City and the tribal offices. The Brown County Historical Society maintains local collections. BLM GLO land patents document original entries. Strong German and Scandinavian immigrant communities left church records from the 1860s. Probate Court records date from 1855. The Hiawatha World newspaper at KSHS has obituaries and legal notices from the 1870s. FamilySearch has microfilmed Brown County deed books, probate records, and marriage records. District Court naturalization records at the courthouse document immigrant arrivals.
county_metadata.online_land_records_scope: BLM GLO federal land patents for Brown County; county deed books at the Register of Deeds office in Hiawatha

county_metadata.jurisdictional_notes: Butler County formed 1855 as an original Kansas Territory county. County seat is El Dorado. Named for Andrew Pickens Butler, US Senator from South Carolina. Large county that spawned several daughter counties. El Dorado oil boom (1915-1920s) transformed the economy. Flint Hills ranching region.
county_metadata.research_tips: Butler County Register of Deeds in El Dorado holds land records from 1855. El Dorado was an oil boom town (1915-1920s) -- oil company and lease records may supplement genealogical research. Butler County Historical Society in El Dorado has local collections. The county's large area spawned several daughter counties (Cowley, Sedgwick, Harvey). BLM GLO land patents document homestead entries. Strong Methodist and Presbyterian congregations have records from the 1860s. Probate Court records date from 1855. Newspapers from El Dorado and Augusta at KSHS. FamilySearch has microfilmed Butler County records. The Flint Hills region has ranching records that may document families -- check KSHS manuscript collections.
county_metadata.online_land_records_scope: BLM GLO federal land patents for Butler County; county deed books at the Register of Deeds office in El Dorado

county_metadata.jurisdictional_notes: Chase County formed 1859 from Butler and Wise (Morris) counties. County seat is Cottonwood Falls. Named for Salmon P. Chase, Secretary of the Treasury and later Chief Justice. Heart of the Flint Hills tallgrass prairie. Cattle ranching has been the primary economy. Cottonwood Falls courthouse (1873) is the oldest in Kansas still in use.
county_metadata.research_tips: Chase County Register of Deeds in Cottonwood Falls holds land records from 1859. Chase County is in the heart of the Flint Hills tallgrass prairie region -- ranching records at KSHS and the Chase County Historical Society are important. The Spring Hill Ranch (Z Bar Ranch) is a National Historic Landmark with records at the National Park Service. The county had significant cattle operations from the 1860s. BLM GLO land patents. Probate Court records from 1859. Church records from early Congregational and Methodist churches. The Chase County Leader newspaper (from 1871) at KSHS. FamilySearch has microfilmed county records. Strong Knapp community records document early settlers.
county_metadata.online_land_records_scope: BLM GLO federal land patents for Chase County; county deed books at the Register of Deeds office in Cottonwood Falls

source_profiles.source_name: Chase County Federal Land Patents -- BLM GLO
source_profiles.repository: Bureau of Land Management, General Land Office Records
source_profiles.coverage_notes: Federal land patents (homestead, preemption, timber culture, cash entry) for Chase County; Kansas is a public land state and GLO patents document original federal land transfers to private ownership

source_profiles.source_name: Chase County Historic Newspapers -- KSHS
source_profiles.repository: Kansas State Historical Society, Topeka
source_profiles.coverage_notes: Historic newspapers for Chase County towns at KSHS on microfilm; legal notices, homestead proof notices, obituaries, marriage announcements, and community news; essential for documenting early settlement in western Kansas

source_profiles.source_name: Chase County Marriage Records -- Probate Court / KSHS / FamilySearch
source_profiles.repository: Chase County Probate Court, Cottonwood Falls / Kansas State Historical Society, Topeka
source_profiles.coverage_notes: Marriage licenses and records for Chase County from 1859; Probate Court issued marriage licenses in Kansas; KSHS holds some county marriage records on microfilm; FamilySearch has indexed Kansas marriages; state vital records registration began 1911

county_metadata.jurisdictional_notes: Chautauqua County formed 1875 from the abolished Howard County. County seat is Sedan. Named for Chautauqua County, New York. Located in the Osage Cuestas region of southeastern Kansas. Ranching and oil production. Sparse population in a rugged terrain.
county_metadata.research_tips: Chautauqua County Register of Deeds in Sedan holds land records from 1875. Formed from the abolished Howard County, researchers should check Howard County records at KSHS for the pre-1875 period. The Chautauqua County Historical Society and Museum in Sedan has local genealogical collections. BLM GLO land patents. The county is in the Osage Cuestas region with ties to the Osage Nation -- check NARA for Osage agency records. Probate Court records from 1875. Church records from Methodist and Baptist congregations. Newspapers at KSHS from the 1880s. FamilySearch has microfilmed some Chautauqua County records. Peru and Sedan cemetery records have been transcribed by local societies.
county_metadata.online_land_records_scope: BLM GLO federal land patents for Chautauqua County; county deed books at the Register of Deeds office in Sedan

source_profiles.source_name: Chautauqua County Deed Books -- Register of Deeds
source_profiles.repository: Chautauqua County Register of Deeds, Sedan
source_profiles.coverage_notes: Original deed books for Chautauqua County from 1875; grantor/grantee indexes; mortgages; liens; Register of Deeds office maintains land records
source_profiles.survival_notes: Records begin from practical settlement in the 1875s-1885s

source_profiles.source_name: Chautauqua County Probate Court Records
source_profiles.repository: Chautauqua County Probate Court, Sedan
source_profiles.coverage_notes: Wills, administrations, inventories, guardianships, and estate settlements for Chautauqua County; Probate Court has jurisdiction over estate matters
